speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in New York. You've had-- you were working there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] You're a US citizen now. Did you have any children who were born in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, yes, yes. We had one son, born in Brooklyn, about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four or five years after we came to the United States, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I understand you moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma. Tell me about that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then we had the idea that we should go in business for ourselves. And a-- my friend, Joe Feiner, he went into business and he needed help. And so we tried to help him out. All our friends helped him to go in business. And I lend him some money too, whatever I had in the account. I cleaned out my account and gave it to him. And when he went into business, he moved to-- it was in Huntington, New York. No, actually, Hempstead. That's where he started, and-- and with the idea that-- listen. You know, one day, maybe I might need some help from you. OK. After a while, he-- he wanted to open up another store in Huntington, New York. And as he opened up the store, he found that one of his partners is stealing. And so that was the end of the partnership. But he did have those two stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he calls me up. He said, Harry, please, would you like to come to work for me? I said, listen. I'm working for this man, a very nice person, and-- until I go in business for myself, do something. He said, well, maybe you need to learn something else. And I just listened to it. And before I knew, I became a expert in the-- in the fabric business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so after two years, I did go to work for him. And I went to see my-- my boss. And I told him the whole story. And he said, listen. Go to work for him. He's a friend of yours. That's OK. But anytime, whenever you are unhappy, please, he said, the door is always open for you in my place. And he had about maybe 70 people working for him. And he told me that I can always come back to work for him. I never did need to go back. I learned the business. And after two years, I told Joe-- I said, listen. I'm not gonna work my life for you. I said, I want to go in business for myself. I lend you the money. You lend me money when I need. So that was very good for him. And one day, I was starting to look for a place where I can have a decorating place and so. And he found out about it. And he said, listen, if you're gonna go in business, you're gonna go with me in business and nobody else. And that was the relationship we started. We came here to Oklahoma and found a place to start the wholesale business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of Oklahoma when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, of course-- we came in the 4th of July, 110 degrees or 115 degrees, or whatever it was. It was so hot. We didn't see a human being in the whole city of Tulsa. It was very, very hot. And so I said, well, we got to sell draperies. We came downtown on Main Street. I didn't see a human being. There was this one restaurant that it was in town. We went there. We had our lunch. We came out. I couldn't touch the car. It was like fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the-- that was the first day to be in Oklahoma. But I never lost faith that I can make it. And we did. Did you have more children in Oklahoma?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Oklahoma, we had two more children, in the city of Tulsa. And yes, there was Michael, who is out of town now. He is in-- in Syracuse, New York with his family. And Danny was born also here in Tulsa. And he is married and he has two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have the same business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fabricut, F-A-B-R-I-C-U-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your children involved in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you enjoy spending your time now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't have a special hobby. You know, some people play golf. I don't play golf, play tennis, do anything. I'm-- like, I'm reading a little bit. And I am still involved in the business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other than that, I really-- we live-- we live in Palm Beach, Florida at wintertime. Summertime, we go quite a bit to Europe and to Switzerland. We have a home there too. And-- and I'm here at the lake. I like boating. I have a very nice boat. And the name of the boat is That's the-- the name-- the same name boat that what I came to the United States with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever returned to your hometown in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a-- a few times, because we are doing business with Poland. We are buying some products from them. So whenever I went there, I did go back to the city where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how does that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm not gonna go back there again. It's not-- not a place that I think I should continue going back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is really a very big question for some people, you know? I am a happy guy always. I'm lighthearted in a way, and little jokes, and things like that. It's easy life. But when you lay down on your pillow, your life changes. I do have a lot of-- lot of memories, and many times kicked and beaten up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your Holocaust experiences have influenced your life through these years and decisions you've made in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It influence-- it influenced-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, with-- it's something that you would want to go through. It's not something that-- that is-- you're looking forward. Nobody can even realize, can understand that there is such a thing. Nobody. I mean, I today have a hard time to believe what I went through myself. So you can imagine how hard it is to tell someone about it and to make them understand what went on in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you know, you-- you get up in the morning. They can grab you and put you in a-- in a gas chamber? This is unheard of. It's not unheard of. It's unbelievable that it happened. But it did happen. That's the way it was. So I just wanted to explain this. And it's very hard to explain to people who-- who didn't have anything to do with the Holocaust. Thank you. The tape. Are my microphones on also?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're running.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ursula-- the one I was talking about that I met in Bremen. This here is my son Danny. And this is my daughter-in-law, Sharon. And Sharon has Brandon. That's my grandchild. And we have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello, hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Lauren, our good-looking girl. Beautiful, beautiful girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my family from one son. We have Michael and Cindy out of town, as I mentioned before, and so they cannot be interviewed. And also, Sue, the wife of Alan, she's also out of town, and she has two children. So we have six grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Guterman, now that it is over 50 years since the Holocaust, what message or lesson would you like to leave with the world today and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] first of all, we-- I hope [BACKGROUND NOISE] and I pray that this will never happen again to anyone. Unfortunately, we didn't learn that much from that Holocaust. It does happen today in various places. People die for their beliefs. People die because they express themselves. And it might go on forever that way. I do, however, hope and pray that this will never happen again to that extent that it happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add? Anything we didn't ask you about today on the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot. [LAUGHS] But unfortunately, we have to cut our time. And maybe hopefully next time, we'll have a chance to talk a little more. There's a lot of things that I was not able to make a statement of because of time. But we hope to live a little longer. And hopefully, we'll meet again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: On top, you have here my grandmother. Her name was Golda Guterman. And on the right is Abraham Wolf Guterman. On the bottom, you have two of my uncle-- three of my uncles and my father. One is Max Guterman, one is [NON-ENGLISH] Guterman, one is Yona Guterman, and my father, Chaim Guterman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the house that I was born in-- Konstantynow. The address is 21 [NON-ENGLISH] Street. This was-- this house is probably about over 100 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The yellow house in the center is the second house that I remember living. I was, at that time, about eight years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a-- the picture of myself and my sister, Luba, and the dog was [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is with two of my friends I went to school with. One is [NON-ENGLISH]. And in-- in the middle is myself. And [NON-ENGLISH] is another friend I had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is of my sister, Luba. She was at that time about 15 years ago-- also living in Konstantynow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is a friend that helped me get to-- to Petzenhausen when we ran away from the trains. And he and my uncle Max carried me all the way to the house, where we were resting after that, and were hiding. I don't know his name. I cannot remember his name. But I wish that I could find him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken on our wedding day, walking out of the synagogue in Bremen, Germany, on the 28th of August, 1946. Its now 50 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the house that I was living in the ghetto in 1940 till about 1944. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is one of the barracks that was in Kaufering. I think it was Lager Vier or Lager Eins. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is one of the barracks that I lived in. And there were about 50 people who lived in one of those in Kaufering between 1944 and the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Petzenhausen-- the house that, when I ran away from the train, we walked in and stayed for four days until the Americans came in, and then lived another week or two. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a certificate identification card that I received in July 1950-- 1945-- the 12th of July, in Bremen, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This here is an affidavit in lieu of a passport. When I came to the United States, I could not-- I was not a citizen, and I wanted to leave the country. So-- for a trip to go to Israel. So I made-- I received this affidavit. And it was just as good as a passport at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is an affidavit in lieu of a passport. I received this in New York because I wanted to go to visit my father in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was issued at a time when I was not a citizen yet.
